C. Adapting to new knowledge
An explicit retrieval system allows us to adapt to new
world knowledge simply by modifying the corpus docu-
ments. To demonstrate this ability, we replace the knowl-
edge corpus with a more recent version of Wikipedia cor-
pus after pre-training is done. When the input query is
about a fact where the two corpora disagree, REALM can
change the prediction to reﬂect the updated information,
as exempliﬁed in Table
4. However, even with an ex-
plicit retrieval mechanism, the knowledge-augmented en-
coder will still end up remembering some world knowl-
edge, making the prediction of some input sentences not
updated with the new corpus. (For instance, the model pre-
dicts “ Thatcher” for “
is the prime minister
of United Kingdom. ” on both corpora, perhaps due to
the frequent mention of her name in Wikipedia articles.)
D. Retrieval Utility
The null document ∅ described in Section
3.4 provides a
way to measure the importance of a retrieved document z:
we deﬁne the retrieval utility (RU) of z for the masked
input x as the difference between the log-likelihood of
the knowledge-augmented encoder when conditioning on
z versus on ∅:
RU(z |x) = log p(y |z, x) − log p(y |∅, x). (2)
A negative RU shows that z is less useful for predicting y
than the null document. This could mean that z is irrelevant
to x, but could also mean that the masked tokens in x do
not require world knowledge to predict, or that the world
knowledge is sufﬁciently commonplace it has been baked
into the model’s parameters. In practice, we ﬁnd that RU
increases steadily over the course of pre-training, and is
more predictive of good performance on the downstream
task of Open-QA than even the overall log-likelihood. An
example of how RU behaves over time and across different
settings is in Figure
4.

Encoder or Retriever We ﬁrst aim to determine whether
REALM pre-training improves the retriever or the encoder,
or both. To do so, we can reset the parameters of either
the retriever or the encoder to their baseline state before
REALM pre-training, and feed that into ﬁne-tuning. Reset-
ting both the retriever and encoder reduces the system to
our main baseline, ORQA. We ﬁnd that both the encoder
and retriever beneﬁt from REALM training separately, but
the best result requires both components acting in unison.
Masking scheme We compare our salient span masking
scheme (Section
3.4) with (1) random token masking in-
troduced in BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018) and (2) random
span masking proposed by SpanBERT ( Joshi et al. , 2019).
While such salient span masking has not been shown to
be impactful in previous work with standard BERT train-
ing (
Joshi et al. , 2019), it is crucial for REALM. Intuitively,
the latent variable learning relies heavily on the utility of re-
trieval and is therefore more sensitive to a consistent lear n-
ing signal.
MIPS index refresh rate During pre-training, we run a
parallel process to re-embed corpus documents and rebuild
the MIPS index. This results in one index refresh per ap-
proximately 500 training steps. To demonstrate the impor-
tance of frequent index refreshes, we compare against using
a slower refresh rate. The results in Table
2 suggests that
a stale index can hurt model training, and further reducing
this staleness could offer better optimization.

able to retrieve document to ﬁll in the masked word even

5. Discussion and Related Work
We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-
tences ( Kiros et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018), and para-
graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.
Retrieve-and-edit with learned retrieval In order to
better explain the variance in the input text and en-
able controllable generation,
Guu et al. (2018) proposed
a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with
high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity.

questions.baseline_answer:
If REALM retrieves an irrelevant document \( z \) for a given input \( x \), this impacts the probabilities and the final answer prediction as follows:

1. **Effect on \( p(z|x) \):**  
   The probability \( p(z|x) \), which represents how likely document \( z \) is retrieved given input \( x \), may still assign some probability mass to the irrelevant document. Since \( p(z|x) \) is modeled by a softmax over relevance scores \( f(x,z) \) (the inner product of embeddings), if the model erroneously deems the irrelevant document somewhat relevant, it will allocate non-negligible probability to it. However, during training, gradients encourage increasing scores for documents that improve prediction accuracy and decreasing scores for worse-performing documents. Therefore, irrelevant documents ideally should have low \( p(z|x) \) after learning, but incorrect retrievals can happen [2].

2. **Effect on \( p(y|z,x) \):**  
   The term \( p(y|z,x) \) models the probability of producing the correct answer \( y \) given the input \( x \) and the retrieved document \( z \). If \( z \) is irrelevant, it contains little or no useful information to help correctly predict \( y \), making \( p(y|z,x) \) low. Consequently, an irrelevant document provides poor support for predicting the output [2][4].

3. **Effect on final answer prediction \( p(y|x) \):**  
   The final output probability marginalizes over all retrieved documents:  
   \[
   p(y|x) = \sum_z p(y|z,x) p(z|x)
   \]
   If an irrelevant document \( z \) has a relatively high retrieval probability \( p(z|x) \) but a low \( p(y|z,x) \), the product \( p(y|z,x) p(z|x) \) contributes little or negatively to \( p(y|x) \), lowering the likelihood of predicting the correct answer. More generally, retrieving irrelevant documents reduces the expected support for the correct answer, thus degrading final answer quality [1][2].

Additional insight is given by the retrieval utility (RU), defined as  
\[
RU(z|x) = \log p(y|z,x) - \log p(y|\emptyset, x)
\]
where \(\emptyset\) is a null document representing no useful
questions.orchestrator_answer: If REALM retrieves an irrelevant document, the frequency with which the gold answer appears among the top-5 retrievals decreases, which serves as a key metric for evaluating the retriever's performance [E8][E9]. The evidence shows that retrieving irrelevant documents leads to a lower rate of gold answer occurrence in the retrieval set, but it does not directly express the impact on the probability distribution p(z|x) or give an explicit probabilistic interpretation for the retrieval of irrelevant versus relevant documents [E8][E9][E10]. The likelihood of correctly predicting the answer, as measured by end-to-end performance, will also decrease when relevant documents are not retrieved, but the evidence does not explicitly define how p(y|z,x) or the final prediction is quantitatively affected in such cases [E7][E9][E10]. In summary, retrieving an irrelevant document reduces the probability that the gold answer is among the retrieved candidates, which negatively impacts overall performance, but the supplied evidence does not provide precise details on changes to p(z|x), p(y|z,x), or the final answer probability in this scenario [E7][E8][E9][E10].

questions.baseline_answer:
When interpreting REALM's reported results, it is important to note that the evaluation includes both end-to-end results and a separate metric measuring how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals before any fine-tuning is applied. This latter metric aims to more clearly isolate the contribution of improving the retriever during pre-training. However, the context does not provide details about limitations, uncertainties, or comparisons with other methods, which are crucial for understanding the broader reliability and significance of REALM's results. Therefore, the missing comparisons or potential uncertainties regarding how REALM performs relative to baseline models or other state-of-the-art retrieval-augmented models should be considered when interpreting the findings.

questions.orchestrator_answer: The evidence indicates that, in addition to end-to-end results, REALM reports how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals before any fine-tuning is applied [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. This metric is used to more significantly isolate the contribution of improving the retriever during pre-training, apart from the effects on overall end-to-end performance [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. However, the available evidence notes do not provide exact values or a detailed quantitative breakdown of these retrieval frequencies [E3][E4]. Furthermore, the evidence does not mention direct comparisons to other retrieval-based systems, so the relative positioning of REALM's retriever performance remains uncertain based on the current documentation [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. Therefore, limitations and uncertainties include the absence of detailed numerical reporting and missing direct comparisons to alternative approaches.

questions.baseline_answer:
Claims about REALM's performance or mechanism that involve the effectiveness of the retriever component, specifically how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals and how this metric isolates the contribution of retriever improvements during pre-training, cannot be verified unless supported by specific experimental evidence from the paper. This includes claims about the retriever's improvement prior to fine-tuning as part of the end-to-end system performance. Verification requires data reporting the top-5 retrieval accuracy before fine-tuning and the effects attributed to retriever pre-training, as emphasized in the context [1][2][3][4][5].

questions.orchestrator_answer: Claims about REALM’s performance or mechanism cannot be verified unless the answer cites specific experimental evidence from the paper, such as reported metrics on the frequency of the gold answer appearing in the top-5 retrievals before fine-tuning [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. For instance, stating that the contribution of the retriever component during pre-training is isolated by measuring how often the gold answer appears among the top-5 retrievals is only verifiable if supported by such experimental evidence from the paper [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. Similarly, claims about end-to-end performance or the impact of pre-training on retriever effectiveness require explicit citation of experimentally reported results or metrics from the paper to be considered verified [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5]. Any statement lacking citation to these specific types of experimental evidence should be regarded as unverified according to the available notes [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5].
